items.correct: Five distinct arguments for God's existence: from motion, causation, contingency, gradation of being, and final causes/teleology — collectively forming Aquinas's natural theology, not a single argument.
items.misconception: Aquinas's Five Ways are five steps of one cosmological argument.
items.random_wrong: Aquinas's Five Ways are five Christian sacraments.
items.why: Each Way is a self-standing argument with different structure. Students treat them as a single chain, missing that Aquinas offered multiple independent routes — the failure of one doesn't undermine the others.

items.correct: The absolute oneness and indivisibility of Allah — the most fundamental Islamic belief. Its opposite, shirk (associating partners with God), is the gravest sin.
items.misconception: Tawhid is the Islamic word for the Trinity.
items.random_wrong: Tawhid is the name of an Islamic prayer position.
items.why: Tawhid is the strict denial of any plurality in God — directly contrasting with Trinitarian Christianity. Students sometimes invert the meaning entirely. The Quran's emphasis on Tawhid shapes Islamic theology, art (no images) and ethics.

items.q: Who do Sikhs consider their living guru today?
items.correct: The Guru Granth Sahib — the Sikh scripture, declared the eternal guru by Guru Gobind Singh after the line of ten human gurus ended.
items.misconception: Sikhs follow a current living guru as their leader.
items.random_wrong: Sikhs consider the Dalai Lama their current guru.
items.why: The line of human gurus ended in 1708; guruship passed to scripture. Students assume a continuing line of personal teachers — missing the distinctive Sikh teaching about the eternal guru in the text.

items.q: Whose 1902 Gifford Lectures examined mystical experience?
items.why: The answer examiners want is: William James. “Rudolf Otto” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.q: Which 16th-century Spanish mystic wrote Interior Castle?
items.why: The answer examiners want is: Teresa of Avila. “John of the Cross” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.q: In what year did Otto publish Das Heilige?
items.why: The real figure is 1917 AD. The wrong options cluster deliberately close — anchor the exact number to one concrete fact so it can't drift.
